findings.summary: Every expense line is a frozen percentage of revenue in all eight years, so the two shipped workbooks show all six line items and net income growing by exactly the same 12.00% year over year, which no real P&L does.
findings.evidence: Put the corpus's only two financial documents side by side. FY2023 -> FY2024: Revenue 2,125,000 -> 2,380,000 (+12.0000%); Compensation 935,425 -> 1,047,676 (+12.0000%); Office & Facilities 211,225 -> 236,572 (+12.0000%); Travel 135,787 -> 152,082 (+12.0000%); Professional Services 120,700 -> 135,184 (+12.0000%); Other 105,613 -> 118,286 (+12.0000%); Net income 616,250 -> 690,200 (+12.0000%). Even 'Other', a grab-bag category, lands on the same rate to the dollar. Across the full ledger every line is a fixed share of revenue in all eight years: Compensation 44.020%, Office & Facilities 9.940%, Travel 6.390%, Professional Services 5.680%, Other 4.970% -- identical to three decimals in 2018 and in 2025 (share spread < 1.4e-6). The firm has no year where it underspent, overspent, or made a choice.

findings.summary: Office & Facilities compounds 11% a year to +89% while the firm's own ground truth says the same five people sat in the same office the whole time; rent is a step-fixed lease cost and cannot track revenue.
findings.evidence: foundation.json pins both sides of the contradiction. Timeline: '2019-08-15: Firm moved into its current office.' Roster: the last hire is Briana Dennis, start '2019-04-06', and every person has 'end': null, so headcount is 5 from April 2019 through 2025 with no hires and no departures. Yet Office & Facilities runs 139,160 (2019) -> 263,608 (2025), +89.4%, reaching $52,722 per head per year ($21,967/month) for five people who never moved and never grew. A commercial lease steps at renewal; it does not compound 11.0% every year for six years because fees went up. Compensation has the same shape (616,280 -> 1,167,410, +89%, an identical 11%/yr raise for all five named individuals every year for seven years), but comp at least has a profit-share reading; facilities has none.

findings.summary: Travel spend rose 9.86% in 2020 for a boutique whose work is on-site diligence, the one year business travel stopped; the ledger has no trace of the pandemic in any line.
findings.evidence: finance.json Travel: 2019 $89,460 -> 2020 $98,278 (+9.86%), then 2021 $108,630 (+10.5%), each exactly 6.390% of revenue. 2020 revenue growing ~10% is defensible on its own (valuation work held up on impairment and 409A demand), so the tell is not the top line, it is that a transaction-advisory firm sending three people to client sites increased travel by a tenth in 2020 and the mix never flinched. Reachable only from the ledger, since no FY2020 workbook ships, but the corpus does narrate 2020 (d:0001-d:0006, the Woods LLC engagement), and any future document or query over FY2020 inherits it. Same root cause as rf:finance-1: expenses are a fixed fraction of revenue, so no line can respond to an event.
